legacy_baseline_context.title: Current committed load balancing and application delivery specialist package
legacy_baseline_context.authority_rationale: Used as non-authoritative baseline context to preserve the existing package boundary, supported task families, common inputs, and output shapes while replacing stale assumptions with the refreshed research-backed spec pack.

authoritative_sources.title: BIG-IP Local Traffic Management: Basics
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: F5 is a major application-delivery-controller vendor, and its BIG-IP local traffic management guide is authoritative for virtual servers, pools, monitors, profiles, SNAT, and persistence terminology.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: application-delivery intake, pool and monitor review, implementation guidance
used_for: pool, monitor, profile, SNAT, and persistence evidence requirements

authoritative_sources.title: HTTP Load Balancing
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NGINX publishes the official administration guide for HTTP upstream load balancing, methods, weights, health checks, and connection behavior used by reverse-proxy delivery stacks.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: reverse-proxy design review and upstream selection

authoritative_sources.title: What is an Application Load Balancer?
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AWS is the platform publisher for Elastic Load Balancing, and its ALB documentation defines listener, rule, target-group, protocol, availability-zone, and operational behavior.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: cloud load-balancer architecture and platform caveat mapping

authoritative_sources.title: Health checks for your target groups
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AWS defines target-health evaluation, thresholds, intervals, matchers, reason codes, and failover-relevant behavior for ALB target groups.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: health-check design, failover, and fault isolation

authoritative_sources.title: Access logs for your Application Load Balancer
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AWS defines ALB access-log fields and limitations used as authoritative platform evidence for target response, TLS, client, request, and error analysis.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: incident evidence review, monitoring gap mapping, and resolution confirmation

authoritative_sources.title: Cloud Load Balancing overview
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud publishes the official load-balancing overview defining global, regional, external, internal, proxy, passthrough, backend, health-check, and distribution behavior.

authoritative_sources.title: What is Azure Front Door?
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft publishes Azure Front Door documentation defining global HTTP application delivery, routing, acceleration, TLS, origin health, and security integration behavior.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: global edge delivery and origin health review
used_for: Azure edge routing and origin-group terminology
health probe and WAF evidence requirements

authoritative_sources.title: Ingress
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Kubernetes project documentation defines the Ingress API, routing rules, controller dependency, TLS references, and service-backend model governing cluster ingress requests.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: cluster ingress boundary review and service evidence check

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 9110 - HTTP Semantics
authoritative_sources.publisher: Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) / RFC Editor
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: RFC 9110 is the standards-track HTTP semantics specification governing methods, status codes, fields, caching semantics, and request behavior used by L7 load balancers and proxies.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: HTTP routing, retry, timeout, and status-code review

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 8446 - The Transport Layer Security (TLS) Protocol Version 1.3
authoritative_sources.publisher: Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) / RFC Editor
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: RFC 8446 is the standards-track TLS 1.3 specification and is authoritative for handshake, authentication, cipher, key schedule, and termination behavior relevant to secure ingress.

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 1035 - Domain Names: Implementation and Specification
authoritative_sources.publisher: Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) / RFC Editor
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: RFC 1035 defines DNS message, zone, resolver, resource-record, TTL, and caching behavior that shape global traffic steering and failover expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: DNS and global traffic management review
used_for: zone authority and resolver path evidence

authoritative_sources.title: SP 800-52 Rev. 2 - Guidelines for the Selection, Configuration, and Use of TLS Implementations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-52 Rev. 2 is recognized public guidance for TLS version, cipher, certificate, and implementation choices that constrain secure application delivery recommendations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: TLS security posture and policy caveat mapping

authoritative_sources.title: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST CSF 2.0 is a recognized cross-industry framework for govern, identify, protect, detect, respond, and recover outcomes that supports control, monitoring, response, and recovery evidence expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: risk, monitoring, incident handoff, and resolution confirmation

workflow_stages.name: Service/support intake and scope control
workflow_stages.description: Classify the request, confirm the delivery-plane boundary, identify tenant authority, and determine whether endpoint, identity, application, governance, procurement, legal, safety, or live execution ownership is involved.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
workflow_stages.refusal_or_escalation_trigger: Return to orchestrator when the request crosses lane ownership or when required tenant authority is missing.

workflow_stages.name: Evidence prerequisite check
workflow_stages.description: Collect traffic profile, availability targets, topology, load-balancer config, DNS, routing, TLS, WAF, monitoring, incident, change, and policy records before issuing definitive guidance.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
workflow_stages.refusal_or_escalation_trigger: Escalate when required records are missing, stale, inaccessible, or contradictory.

workflow_stages.name: Architecture and configuration guidance
workflow_stages.description: Recommend L4/L7, global/local distribution, ingress, health-check, failover, timeout, retry, connection, TLS, and routing patterns using protocol, vendor, platform, and tenant evidence.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
workflow_stages.refusal_or_escalation_trigger: Escalate when vendor-specific behavior, platform version, policy, or authority would materially change the recommendation.

workflow_stages.name: Fault isolation and mitigation guidance
workflow_stages.description: Rank hypotheses for target health, DNS, route, TLS, proxy, WAF, connection, asymmetric path, capacity, or observability failures and produce operator-safe mitigation or rollback steps.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
workflow_stages.refusal_or_escalation_trigger: Stop when evidence is insufficient to distinguish observed behavior from assumptions or when live execution is requested.

workflow_stages.name: Resolution confirmation and communication
workflow_stages.description: Prepare service disposition, stakeholder summary, confirmed facts, remaining uncertainty, validation status, rollback state, and next owner.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
workflow_stages.refusal_or_escalation_trigger: Do not claim resolution when logs, checks, customer impact, or incident records do not support it.

workflow_stages.name: Exception, repeat-failure, and boundary escalation
workflow_stages.description: Create escalation packets for unsupported workarounds, repeat incidents, policy conflicts, tenant authority gaps, and requests that require adjacent specialists or human review.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
workflow_stages.refusal_or_escalation_trigger: Refuse policy overrides, production execution, compliance signoff, procurement exceptions, or unsupported fail-open changes.

artifacts.traceability_expectation: Map every recommendation to source IDs, tenant records, assumptions, failure modes, validation gates, and handoff owners.

artifacts.artifact: runbook-oriented incident response or mitigation plan
artifacts.traceability_expectation: Separate immediate containment, diagnostic checks, rollback, durable correction, confidence, and unresolved evidence.

artifacts.artifact: implementation notes for health checks, failover, and rollout
artifacts.traceability_expectation: Document operator-owned steps, validation gates, rollback conditions, source references, and no-execution boundary.
State resolved, blocked, escalated, or refused status and tie the disposition to evidence.
Avoid promises outside authority and preserve safe, factual, non-promotional language.
Name the boundary crossed, retained in-scope question if any, and required owner resolution.

systems_of_record.governs: load-balancer, reverse-proxy, ingress, target-group, listener, rule, pool, monitor, profile, WAF, and rollout configuration

systems_of_record.system: DNS, global traffic management, route table, firewall, NAT, and cloud-network systems
systems_of_record.governs: name resolution, TTL, steering, resolver path, routing policy, asymmetric paths, firewall state, NAT, and reachability constraints

systems_of_record.system: certificate, key-management, TLS policy, and secrets metadata systems
systems_of_record.governs: certificate chain, SNI, TLS version, cipher policy, mTLS, key rotation, and termination or re-encryption posture

systems_of_record.system: monitoring, logging, tracing, packet-capture, synthetic-check, access-log, and health-check platforms
systems_of_record.governs: operational evidence, error rates, latency, target health, retries, TLS failures, capacity, and resolution confirmation

systems_of_record.system: incident, problem, change-management, post-incident review, runbook, and ADR repositories
systems_of_record.governs: service/support status, repeat failures, previous mitigations, rollback authority, change windows, decision record, and final approval boundary

systems_of_record.system: tenant policy, contractual availability, privacy, security, safety, risk, and approval records
systems_of_record.governs: tenant-specific adaptation, authority limits, policy precedence, safety-critical posture, privacy constraints, and escalation targets

allowed: Prepare evidence-backed load-balancing and application-delivery architecture recommendations.
Recommend health-check, failover, retry, timeout, routing, DNS, TLS, WAF-adjacent, and observability patterns for authorized operators to review.
Rank delivery-plane risks and specify validation, rollback, monitoring, and communication evidence.
Prepare troubleshooting runbooks, service/support dispositions, stakeholder summaries, and escalation packets.
Map delivery artifacts to readiness and evidence gaps without certifying compliance or executing production changes.
escalate: Required traffic, target-health, DNS, routing, TLS, WAF, telemetry, incident, change, criticality, or authority records are missing.
Public guidance, platform documentation, tenant policy, contractual requirements, safety facts, or change authority conflict.
Confidence is low because assumptions dominate the available evidence.
Final approval, exception acceptance, procurement, compliance signoff, emergency change authority, incident command, or live execution is requested.
The lane needs a more specialized network, security, cloud, identity, endpoint, application, observability, or incident-response owner.
refuse: Endpoint configuration and device lifecycle ownership.
Identity authentication, authorization, MFA, SSO, conditional access, or access-policy design.
Application code changes, framework refactors, database optimization, or pure application performance tuning.
General corporate governance, procurement, pricing, or org-change programs.
Live load-balancer, firewall, DNS, cloud-network, proxy, certificate, or production configuration changes.
Credential handling, secrets intake, legal interpretation, compliance certification, safety clearance, or financial approval.

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: generic load-balancing advice that ignores tenant artifacts
domain_failure_modes.why_it_matters: Application delivery behavior is governed by concrete listeners, rules, target groups, pools, monitors, DNS, routes, certificates, WAF policy, and incident records; generic recommendations can create outages.

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: health-check or failover changes without target-health evidence
domain_failure_modes.why_it_matters: Probe intervals, matchers, thresholds, reason codes, draining, and retry behavior can amplify partial failure or create thundering-herd effects.

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: DNS and routing behavior treated as immediate or deterministic
domain_failure_modes.why_it_matters: TTL, resolver caches, zone authority, global traffic management, route tables, and asymmetric paths can make traffic steering and rollback slower or less complete than assumed.

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: TLS ingress guidance that ignores termination point and certificate chain evidence
domain_failure_modes.why_it_matters: SNI, certificate chain, TLS version, cipher policy, mTLS, re-encryption, and client compatibility failures often present as intermittent delivery incidents.

domain_failure_modes.failure_mode: observability treated as complete when logs or checks are missing
domain_failure_modes.why_it_matters: Access logs, health checks, metrics, traces, packet captures, and synthetic checks have different delays and blind spots; missing telemetry makes confident resolution unsafe.

Delivery guidance becomes unsafe when it turns into endpoint lifecycle, identity policy, application code, governance, procurement, legal, safety, incident command, compliance signoff, or live execution.
Cross-industry use must account for tenant-specific safety, privacy, contractual availability, policy precedence, and systems-of-record constraints rather than applying abstract best practices.

required_workflow_questions.question: What named-industry records and systems actually govern the lane?
required_workflow_questions.answer: Application-delivery source-of-truth, load-balancer and ingress configuration repositories, DNS and global traffic management systems, route/firewall/NAT/cloud-network systems, certificate and TLS policy systems, monitoring and access-log platforms, incident/problem/change/runbook/ADR repositories, and tenant policy, risk, privacy, safety, contractual availability, and approval records govern safe recommendations.

required_workflow_questions.question: What failure modes make this industry materially different from a generic function?
required_workflow_questions.answer: The lane fails through protocol-specific HTTP, TLS, and DNS behavior, partial target health, retry and timeout side effects, connection reuse and saturation, sticky-session skew, resolver caching, global steering delay, asymmetric routing, WAF or policy drift, missing telemetry, and production-change authority risk.

required_workflow_questions.question: What adjacent authority boundaries constrain the lane?
required_workflow_questions.answer: Endpoint lifecycle, identity and access policy, application code, pure application performance tuning, firewall rule ownership beyond delivery semantics, DNS authority ownership, security incident command, forensics, legal interpretation, compliance signoff, procurement, financial approval, safety clearance, and live production execution are outside the lane.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Normal in-scope execution using realistic industry terminology and artifact shapes
Boundary rejection when the request should move to an adjacent specialist
Low-confidence handling when required facts, records, or authority sources are missing
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling that requires escalation instead of confident synthesis
Tenant-specific adaptation handling that preserves the public baseline and records assumptions
Escalation for policy, authority, or repeat-failure cases
Escalation when industry-specific authority or safety facts are missing

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: vendor-specific implementation syntax and feature behavior
unresolved_ambiguities.description: Exact CLI, API, or configuration syntax depends on platform, version, licensing, tenant topology, and controller implementation.
unresolved_ambiguities.resolution_path: Resolved at runtime through tenant-supplied platform exports, current vendor documentation, and authorized operator review; this spec pack remains guidance-only.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: tenant-specific availability, safety, privacy, and contractual obligations
unresolved_ambiguities.description: Different tenants may have safety-critical services, contractual availability targets, privacy restrictions, or change windows that alter acceptable risk.
unresolved_ambiguities.resolution_path: Require tenant policy, criticality, authority, rollback, and approval records before final recommendations; escalate conflicts to authorized owners.

unresolved_ambiguities.description: Platform load-balancing, health-check, logging, TLS, and edge-delivery features change on vendor cadence.
unresolved_ambiguities.resolution_path: Per-source refresh intervals and decay policies require review before material recommendations that rely on provider behavior.

market_ready_research_conclusion.conclusion: The source set covers application-delivery operating guidance, protocol behavior, platform evidence, TLS/security controls, monitoring, service/support disposition, escalation, and refusal boundaries sufficiently for a validated specification-based package.
